note: Script-lock revision. The four pre-lock follow-ups flagged in qc.v001 (OIG/whistleblower detail, $65B/$17.5B definitions + latest recovery, named institutional victims, exact 11-count wording) were confirmed against primary / primary-equivalent sources on 2026-06-19. All claims are now approved; none are unsupported. IMPORTANT: confirming these facts did NOT change any wording in script v001 (the script states qualitative/range phrasing, not the exact figures updated here), so NO script v002 is required and APR-0003 stands. R1: defendant deceased and pleaded guilty, so adjudicated facts can be stated plainly; trustee/OIG findings still attributed to source; non-defendants described neutrally.
changes_from_v001: CLM-0001: added exact verbatim 11-count list (incl. 'Making A False Filing With The SEC' and the three money-laundering counts that the v001 parenthetical omitted); source SRC-0013 added.

CLM-0005: locked the OIG finding (six substantive complaints, June 1992-Dec 2008) and the Markopolos submission years (2000, 2001, 2005, 2007, 2008) and the verified title/date of the 2005 memo; sources SRC-0011 (now verified), SRC-0012 added.
CLM-0008: added sourced, neutrally-framed named institutional victims; status approved.
CLM-0009: updated to the latest officially confirmed recovery figures (as of 2025-10-31) and DOJ MVF final distribution; sources SRC-0014, SRC-0015 added.
CLM-0011: status needs_primary_confirm -> approved; locked OIG report number/date/IG and the 'failure not corruption' framing; SRC-0011 now verified.

claims.normalized_claim: On March 12, 2009, Bernard L. Madoff pleaded guilty in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York to an 11-count criminal information and was remanded into custody. The eleven counts were: (1) Securities Fraud; (2) Investment Adviser Fraud; (3) Mail Fraud; (4) Wire Fraud; (5) International Money Laundering To Promote Specified Unlawful Activity; (6) International Money Laundering To Conceal And Disguise The Proceeds Of Specified Unlawful Activity; (7) Money Laundering; (8) False Statements; (9) Perjury; (10) Making A False Filing With The Securities and Exchange Commission; (11) Theft From An Employee Benefit Plan.
verbatim_counts: International Money Laundering To Promote Specified Unlawful Activity
International Money Laundering To Conceal And Disguise The Proceeds Of Specified Unlawful Activity
Making A False Filing With The Securities and Exchange Commission
Theft From An Employee Benefit Plan
allowed_wording: he pleaded guilty to eleven federal felonies
he admitted it in open court
prohibited_wording: he was found guilty by a jury
claims.notes: He PLEADED guilty (no trial). Do not say 'convicted by a jury'. The 11 counts are now exact (verbatim_counts); the script need only say 'eleven felonies' — the full list is for citation lower-thirds / description if used.

claims.normalized_claim: On June 29, 2009, Madoff was sentenced to 150 years in federal prison, the statutory maximum, and later held at a Federal Correctional Complex in North Carolina.
allowed_wording: he was sentenced to 150 years
the maximum sentence

claims.normalized_claim: Madoff's investment advisory business operated as a Ponzi scheme: rather than investing client money, he deposited it in a bank account and paid requested 'returns' and withdrawals out of other investors' principal; investigators and the trustee found the advisory arm executed essentially no real securities trades for years and produced fabricated account statements and trade confirmations.
allowed_wording: he paid old investors with new investors' money
there were almost no real trades — the statements were fabricated
prohibited_wording: every part of his firm was fake (the separate market-making arm was a real business)
claims.notes: THE mechanism / spine. Distinguish the fraudulent advisory arm from Madoff's legitimate market-making/broker-dealer business.

claims.normalized_claim: The widely cited '$65 billion' figure is the total fabricated value reported across customer account statements (paper value including fictional profits), not the cash that went in; the trustee has estimated actual lost principal at approximately $17.5 billion. A separate '$170 billion' figure cited at sentencing reflects funds that flowed through the accounts (forfeiture), not net investor loss.
allowed_wording: the famous $65 billion was mostly money that never really existed — fictional profits on paper
the real cash lost was about $17.5 billion
prohibited_wording: investors handed over $65 billion in cash
$65 billion was stolen and is gone
claims.notes: MYTH-CORRECTION payoff. $65B = fabricated statement value; ~$17.5B = principal; do not present $170B as 'the amount stolen' (see contradictions CON-01).

claims.normalized_claim: Financial analyst Harry Markopolos repeatedly warned the SEC that Madoff's returns were mathematically impossible, submitting concerns in 2000, 2001, 2005, 2007 and 2008; his November 7, 2005 submission was titled 'The World's Largest Hedge Fund Is a Fraud' and laid out roughly 30 red flags. The SEC's Office of Inspector General (Report No. OIG-509) later found the SEC received six substantive complaints about Madoff between June 1992 and December 2008 but never substantiated them through a competent examination, and the scheme was not stopped before its 2008 collapse.
allowed_wording: a single analyst warned the SEC for years that the math was impossible
his repeated red-flag reports went unheeded
he warned them in 2000, 2001, 2005, 2007 and 2008
prohibited_wording: no one ever tried to warn anyone
claims.notes: HUMAN SPINE. Locked to OIG-509 (six substantive complaints, Jun 1992-Dec 2008) and the verified 2005 memo title/date (SRC-0012, primary OIG exhibit). The script uses qualitative phrasing ('for years', 'a financial analyst') and needs no change; exact years/title available for citation lower-thirds.

claims.normalized_claim: A central red flag was that Madoff's reported returns were unusually smooth and consistent — steady positive gains across years and across up and down markets — a pattern that is implausible for real investing and was the statistical tell the whistleblower relied on.
allowed_wording: returns too steady to be real
a line that almost never went down — and that was the warning sign
claims.notes: THE takeaway / thumbnail concept. Frame as the red flag in THIS case; explicitly NOT investment advice. Some interpretive content -> grade B, use measured wording.

claims.normalized_claim: The scheme unraveled during the 2008 financial crisis when investors sought to withdraw billions and Madoff could not meet redemptions; he confessed to his sons, who reported him to authorities, and he was arrested on December 11, 2008.
allowed_wording: the 2008 panic triggered a wave of withdrawals he couldn't cover
his sons reported him and he was arrested in December 2008
prohibited_wording: his sons were part of the fraud (they were not charged)
claims.notes: Do not imply the sons were complicit; neither was criminally charged. Describe neutrally.

claims.normalized_claim: Madoff's victims were not only individuals but also sophisticated institutions — banks, hedge funds and feeder funds, universities, and charities — some of which were forced to close. Documented institutional exposure included the Fairfield Greenwich Group (a major feeder fund, on the order of $7 billion routed to Madoff), the Tremont Group (a MassMutual subsidiary, on the order of $3 billion), international banks such as Banco Santander (via its Optimal fund) and HSBC, university endowments such as Yeshiva University, and charities such as the JEHT Foundation, which closed. The breadth of expert victims underlines that a trusted name and smooth returns fooled professionals, not just amateurs.
allowed_wording: his victims included banks, charities and professional funds
even sophisticated investors were fooled
feeder funds, universities and charities were among the casualties
prohibited_wording: name specific living victims as foolish
imply any named feeder fund was knowingly complicit (some settled civil clawback claims without admission)
claims.notes: Supports the 'smartest people fell for it' beat (angle element C). Named institutions are illustrative and neutral (exposure/loss, not complicity); figures are widely reported magnitudes, not exact court-fixed losses — phrase as 'on the order of'. Keep neutral; do not single out or mock named victims.

claims.normalized_claim: Court-appointed SIPA trustee Irving Picard has recovered the large majority of the lost principal through settlements and clawbacks. As of October 31, 2025 the trustee had recovered or reached agreements to recover approximately $14.83 billion, and had distributed approximately $14.67 billion to BLMIS customers — equal to 72.015% of each allowed claim. Combined with the separate DOJ Madoff Victim Fund (which completed its final distribution in early 2025), many investors have recovered well over 90% of their actual losses.
allowed_wording: most of the real money was eventually clawed back
many victims recovered the large majority of what they put in
well over ninety percent for many investors
prohibited_wording: everyone got all their money back
claims.verify_note: Distributions are ongoing; figures above are the latest OFFICIALLY confirmed (SIPC release, as of 2025-10-31: ~$14.83B recovered, ~$14.67B distributed, 72.015%). Later 2026 reporting cites higher totals (~$15.3B+); re-pull the latest distribution release from SIPC / madofftrustee.com at publish time and cite the figure current on the publish date. The script's 'well over ninety percent' is qualitative and remains supported.
claims.notes: Surprising, under-told beat (recovery). Use ranges/qualitative in narration; reserve exact figures for citation lower-thirds with the as-of date.

claims.normalized_claim: Bernard Madoff died in federal prison on April 14, 2021, at age 82, having served roughly 12 years of his sentence.
allowed_wording: he died in prison in 2021

claims.normalized_claim: The SEC's Office of Inspector General examined the agency's failure to uncover the scheme despite credible, specific complaints over many years. In Report No. OIG-509, 'Investigation of Failure of the SEC to Uncover Bernard Madoff's Ponzi Scheme' (Inspector General H. David Kotz, August 31, 2009), the OIG concluded the SEC had received but never substantiated the allegations through a competent examination. The OIG found no evidence that any SEC personnel had a financial or other improper connection with Madoff, and found that a former official's family relationship to the Madoff family did not influence the examinations — i.e., the finding was failure/incompetence, not corruption.
allowed_wording: the SEC's own watchdog later found the agency missed repeated warnings
an internal report concluded the SEC never properly investigated the tips
claims.notes: Attribute to OIG-509. Do NOT allege corruption — the OIG expressly found no improper financial connection and that the family relationship did not influence the exams; the finding was failure/incompetence.
